steps.prompt: Which word is the verb in the relative clause?

hints.humiliabitur: 'Humiliabitur' is the main clause verb, not the relative clause verb

hints.to go out: 'To go out' is 'exire' — 'exaltare' means 'to raise up'

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'exaltet' — 'exaltat' ends in -at (indicative)

hints.exeo, exire, exii, exitum: These are the parts of 'exire' (to go out)
hints.exigo, exigere, exegi, exactum: These are the parts of 'exigere' (to demand/drive out)
hints.tollo, tollere, sustuli, sublatum: These are the parts of 'tollere' (to lift up) — a classical synonym

hints.to be human: 'To be human' is not a standard Latin verb — 'humiliari' means 'to be humbled'
hints.to be buried: 'To be buried' is 'sepeliri' or 'humari' — 'humiliari' means 'to be humbled'

steps.prompt: What tense, mood, and voice is 'humiliabitur'?

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would be 'humiliatur' — 'humiliabitur' has future -bi-
hints.future active indicative: Future active would be 'humiliabit' — the -tur ending marks passive
hints.perfect passive indicative: Perfect passive would be 'humiliatus est' — 'humiliabitur' is future

hints.humo, humare, humavi, humatum: These are the parts of 'humare' (to bury) — a different verb
hints.humeo, humere, —, —: These are the parts of 'humere' (to be moist) — unrelated
hints.inicio, inicere, inieci, iniectum: These are the parts of 'inicere' (to throw in) — unrelated

hints.nominative: Reflexive 'se' has no nominative form — it is accusative here as direct object
hints.ablative: 'Se' can be ablative, but as direct object of 'exaltat' it is accusative
hints.dative: Dative reflexive would be 'sibi' — 'se' is accusative

translation: Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled
